content.problemStatement:
We call a permutation‚Ä†^{\dag}‚Ä† PPP of length NNN as KKK-sortable if it is possible to **sort** the permutation (in non-decreasing order) using the following operation **at most** once :


Choose a subset of indices from [1,N][1, N][1,N];
Add KKK to the elements present at all those indices in PPP.
Given NNN and a permutation PPP of length NNN, find the **sum** of all KKK where:


1‚Č§K‚Č§N1\le K\le N1‚Č§K‚Č§N;
PPP is KKK-sortable.
‚Ä†^{\dag}‚Ä† A permutation of length NNN is an array of NNN distinct elements which are all in the range [1,N][1, N][1,N]. For example, [2,3,1,4][2, 3, 1, 4][2,3,1,4] is a permutation of length 444 whereas [2,4,3][2, 4, 3][2,4,3] and [1,1,2][1, 1, 2][1,1,2] are not


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains one integer, NNN - the length of the permutation.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers, P1,P2,‚Ä¶,PNP_1, P_2, \ldots, P_NP1‚Äč,P2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,PN‚Äč - the permutation PPP.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, sum of all KKK such that PPP is KKK-sortable and 1‚Č§K‚Č§N1 \le K \le N1‚Č§K‚Č§N.
constraints: Pi‚Č†PjP_i \ne P_jPi‚ÄčÓÄ†=Pj‚Äč for all 1‚Č§i<j‚Č§N1 \le i \lt j \le N1‚Č§i<j‚Č§N.
The sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 5‚čÖ1055 \cdot 10^55‚čÖ105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 1 : The permutation P=[2,1]P = [2, 1]P=[2,1] is both 111-sortable and 222-sortable.

K=1K = 1K=1 : Choose subset = [2][2][2], and add KKK to the 2nd2^{nd}2nd position, PPP becomes [2,2][2, 2][2,2].
K=2K = 2K=2 : Choose subset = [2][2][2], and add KKK to the 2nd2^{nd}2nd position, PPP becomes [2,3][2, 3][2,3].
